critique.summary: The article discusses Trevor Milton's comeback after receiving a presidential pardon from Donald Trump for his conviction of defrauding investors in Nikola, a hydrogen truck company. Milton is now raising funds for a new jet that he claims will transform flying.
mainPoints: Trevor Milton was convicted in 2022 of defrauding investors in Nikola, a hydrogen truck company he founded.
Nikola filed for bankruptcy in early 2025, and Milton faced a prison term and restitution demands.
In March 2025, former President Donald Trump granted Milton an unconditional pardon.
Post-pardon, Milton is raising funds for a new jet project, claiming it will revolutionize flying.
Milton has been seen mingling with elites and celebrities at high-profile events, signaling a shift in his public status.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about Trevor Milton's conviction, Nikola's bankruptcy, and the presidential pardon by Donald Trump are consistent with publicly available information from reputable news sources. The details about Milton's fundraising for a new jet are less verifiable but are presented as claims made by Milton himself. The article is factual in its reporting of events and legal outcomes, citing specific dates and actions. However, it does not provide independent verification of the new jet's potential or technical details.
considerations: The article does not provide technical or expert analysis of the new jet's feasibility or innovation claims.
There is no mention of perspectives from Nikola investors, industry experts, or regulatory bodies regarding Milton's comeback or new venture.
The article does not explore the broader implications of the pardon on investor confidence or legal precedent.
Potential conflicts of interest or motivations behind the pardon and Milton's public relations efforts are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on skepticism and criticism of Trevor Milton, a convicted fraudster pardoned by Donald Trump, who is now raising funds for a new jet project. Commenters express distrust in Milton's character and question the motivations of investors and media supporting him, while some highlight broader issues of corruption and repeated fraud.

comments.author: nerevarthelame
comments.text:
On top of the fraud convictions, Trevor Milton was credibly accused of sexual assault by his own cousin and a girl he employed. Both victims were minors at the time.

https://www.cnbc.com/2020/09/29/two-women-file-sexual-abuse-...
comments.analysis: This comment provides factual information supported by credible sources, adding important context about Milton's background beyond the fraud conviction.

comments.author: game_the0ry
comments.text:
I struggle to understand the psychology of how founders who are clearly incompetent charlatans get second+ chances -- they couldn't do fraud successfully but investors have a faith they could do business successfully. But they still get funding (like adam neumann of wework fame) and full on "narrative tongue baths" by the business media community (like this wsj article on trevor milton).

Why? I struggle to understand the incentives + motivations here.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid and insightful question about investor psychology and media narratives surrounding controversial founders, reflecting a common skepticism in the startup ecosystem.

comments.author: josefritzishere
comments.text: I cannot fathom the thinking of any party investing in the new company of a convicted fraudster.
comments.analysis: Expresses a reasonable doubt about the rationale behind investing in Milton again, aligning with common concerns about trustworthiness after fraud convictions.

comments.author: close04
comments.text:
> Trevor Milton’s conviction for defrauding investors in truck company Nikola was wiped away

Best justice money can buy.

> He’s now raising funds for a new jet he claims will transform flying

With his history building the "truck that can roll unpowered down a hill" I shudder to think just how his jet would transform flying.
comments.analysis: Highlights perceived injustice and cynicism about the pardon and Milton's new venture, though the tone is somewhat biased and speculative.

comments.author: NickC25
comments.text: I'm sorry, but anyone Donald Trump has pardoned for fraud should not be trusted at all.  It's literally a matter of "game recognizes game".  If Donald Trump gets a cut of the fraud, that's all that matters to him.
comments.analysis: Reflects a common viewpoint about Trump's pardons and their implications, though it is a broad generalization without specific evidence in this context.

comments.author: pstuart
comments.text: I love how the article talked about the big claims for improved flight capabilities without a single word describing what that supposed secret sauce may be.
comments.analysis: Critiques the lack of technical detail in the article, a valid point that questions the credibility of the claims made about the new jet.

comments.author: etempleton
comments.text:
In my experience, people who are compulsive liars or those who are willing to make large or repeated deceptions for personal gain never change. It is as natural to them as breathing. Some of them I am quite convinced believe their lies, but the net result is the same.

I don't know Trevor Milton. I have never met him. Maybe he isn't a compulsive liar but just got in over his head and was trying to make it work. But I know I would never invest in something he is doing.
comments.analysis: Offers a psychological perspective on habitual dishonesty, relevant to the discussion about Milton's character and trustworthiness.

comments.author: maxweylandt
comments.analysis: Simply provides a link to the article without additional commentary or analysis, adding little value to the discussion.

comments.author: cat-turner
comments.text: People like to invest in people like them. Simple as that.
comments.analysis: Offers a concise sociological insight into investor behavior, though it is somewhat simplistic and lacks elaboration.

comments.author: JoshGG
comments.text: Says a lot about whether you can trust the WSJ.
comments.analysis: Expresses distrust in the media source with a dismissive tone, but lacks substantive reasoning or evidence.
